perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means, in respect to each Party: (a) any information in which such Party claims a proprietary and/or confidential interest; (b) all confidential matters of such Party including, without limitation, technical kn ow-how, trade secrets, technical data, new product strategy, new products or processes, analysis, compilations, concepts, technical processes, formulae, recipes, specifications, inventions, research projects, customer lists, pricing, pricing policies, operational methods, financial information, marketing information and other business affairs; (c) any information of a confidential nature concerning such Party’s customers, suppliers or employees; and (d) any information such Party has received from othe rs which they are obliged to treat as proprietary and/or confidential .
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means, in respect to each Party: (a) any information in which such Party subjectively deems confidential; (b) all matters such Party chooses to keep private, including but not limited to, technical know-how, data, strategies, processes, concepts, formulae, recipes, specifications, inventions, projects, lists, pricing, methods, financial information, and business affairs; (c) any information concerning such Party’s associates; and (d) any information such Party has received with a verbal agreement to keep private.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' beyond legally recognized trade secrets or proprietary information. By using terms like 'subjectively deems confidential' and 'chooses to keep private,' it removes the requirement for the information to have actual commercial value or be actively protected, conflicting with trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires that a trade secret derive independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified text allows for information with no inherent value to be considered confidential, which is outside the scope of trade secret protection under the UTSA. It removes the requirement for active protection and broadens the definition significantly.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include anything a party 'subjectively deems confidential' or 'chooses to keep private'. This contradicts the established legal understanding, as suggested by the perturbation explanation. The law explanation states that UTSA requires a trade secret to derive independent economic value from not being generally known, and the modified text allows information with no inherent value to be considered confidential. Scraped Law Snippet 1 from 18 USC 1839 provides definitions related to trade secrets, implying a specific legal understanding of what constitutes a trade secret which has not been preserved in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information i n strict confidence and take all reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree o f care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information in confidence to the best of their ability; (ii) use a degree of care they deem appropriate; and (iii) make a reasonable effort to prevent the removal of notices.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a 'reasonable steps' standard, while the modified text weakens it to 'best of their ability' and 'deem appropriate.' This reduces the obligation to actively protect confidential information, creating a loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Changing 'reasonable steps' to 'best of their ability' and allowing the recipient to determine the 'appropriate' level of care undermines the legal standard for protecting trade secrets, potentially exempting them from liability under the Act if their efforts are deemed subjectively sufficient but objectively inadequate.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information. By replacing 'reasonable steps' with 'best of their ability' and 'degree of care they deem appropriate,' the contract deviates from the standard that would be expected to comply with laws such as the Economic Espionage Act. Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. This misalignment with established legal standards for protecting trade secrets creates a legal contradiction, as the modified clause could be interpreted as allowing a lower level of protection than legally required.

perturbation.original_text: All Documents made available hereunder, including all copies, notes, summaries, and abstracts thereof, shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon completion of the Agen cy Sales and Marketing Services with Distributor or upon written request by the Disclosing Party, and Recipient shall certify in writing that it has complied with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents may be returned or destroyed at the Recipient's discretion upon completion of services or upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Certification of compliance is not required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory return or destruction of documents and the certification requirement weakens the protection of confidential information. It gives the Recipient the option to retain the documents even after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the documents contain trade secrets and are knowingly retained with the intent to deprive the owner of their rights, it could potentially violate the National Stolen Property Act if those documents are transported across state lines. By making the return discretionary, the agreement increases the risk of violating this law.

Transportation of stolen goods, securities, moneys, fraudulent State tax stamps, or articles used in counterfeiting Whoever transports, transmits, or transfers in interstate or foreign commerce any goods, wares, merchandise, securities or money, of the value of $5,000 or more, kn
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return or destruction of documents and certification of compliance, which protects confidential information. The changed contract makes the return or destruction discretionary and removes the certification requirement, increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. According to the LLM explanation, retaining trade secrets with the intent to deprive the owner of their rights could potentially violate the National Stolen Property Act, especially if transported across state lines. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 2314, addresses the transportation of stolen goods, which could include trade secrets in document form. The modified contract weakens the protection of these trade secrets, increasing the likelihood of violating 18 USC 2314 if the recipient transports the documents across state lines with the intent to deprive the owner of their rights. The change creates a legal contradiction because it facilitates a scenario that could lead to a violation of federal law.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNE D BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF THE PRINCIPAL PLACE OF BUSINESS OF DISTRIBUTOR AS SET FORTH IN THE FIRST PARAGRAPH ABOVE (REGARDLESS OF THE LAWS THAT MIGHT OTHERWISE GOVERN UNDER APPLICABLE PRINCIPLES OF CONFLICTS OF LAW) AS TO ALL MATTERS, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO MATTERS OF VALIDITY, CONSTRUCTION, EFFECT, PERFORMANCE AND REMEDY.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Delaware regardless of the location of either party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, regardless of the parties' locations, might contradict state laws regarding contract enforcement and consumer protection in the states where the Distributor and Agency actually operate.
perturbation.law_citation: Example: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have consumer protection laws that apply to transactions within their jurisdiction. Specifying Delaware law, even if neither party is based there, could conflict with these state laws if the agreement affects consumers or businesses within those states, potentially voiding provisions that violate local consumer protection statutes. This change limits the ability to seek remedies under the more protective laws of the state where the actual transaction took place.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have. (6) Representing that goods are original or new if they have deteriorated unreasonably or are altered, reconditioned, reclaimed, used, or secondhand. (7) Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if they are of another. (8) Disparaging the goods, services, or business of another by false or misleading representation of fact. (9) Advertising goods or services with intent not to sell them as advertised. (10) Advertising goods or services with intent not to supply reasonably expectable demand, unless the advertisement discloses a limitation of quantity. (11) Advertising furniture without clearly indicating that it is unassembled if that is the case. (12) Advertising the price of unassembled furniture without clearly indicating the assembled price of that furniture if the same furniture is available assembled from the seller. (13) Making false or misleading statements of fact concerning reasons for, existence of, or amounts of, price reductions. (14) Representing that a transaction confers or involves rights, remedies, or obligations that it does not have or involve, or that are prohibited by law. (15) Representing that a part, replacement, or repair service is needed when it is not. (16) Representing that the subject of a transaction has been supplied in accordance with a previous representation when it has not. (17) Representing that the consumer will receive a rebate, discount, or other economic benefit, if the earning of the benefit is contingent on an event to occur subsequent to the consummation of the transaction. (18) Misrepresenting the authority of a salesperson, representative, or agent to negotiate the final terms of a transaction with a consumer. (19) Inserting an unconscionable provision in the contract. (20) Advertising that a product is being offered at a specific price plus a specific percentage of that price unless (A) the
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law as governing, regardless of the parties' locations. This contradicts consumer protection laws in states where the Distributor and Agency operate, as the Law Explanation points out. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1770 (a)(14) of the California Civil Code, prohibits representing that a transaction confers rights, remedies, or obligations that it does not have or that are prohibited by law. If the transaction occurs in California but is governed by Delaware law that offers less protection than California law, the changed text could violate CIV 1770 (a)(14). This is a clear contradiction because it undermines the consumer protections mandated by California law, potentially making the agreement unenforceable in California.
